citeinfo.title: Assessing spatial variability of nutrients, phytoplankton, and related water quality constituents in the California Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta at the landscape scale: 2023 High resolution mapping surveys

descript.abstract: Broad-area, high-resolution, boat-based water quality mapping surveys of the Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta and Suisun Bay (Delta) of California, USA were conducted under different environmental/flow conditions in May, July, and October of 2023. The spatial and temporal variability of nutrients, phytoplankton, and related water quality parameters were assessed. This dataset includes measurements of nitrate, ammonium, orthophosphate, dissolved organic carbon, temperature, conductivity, dissolved oxygen, and chlorophyll.
descript.purpose: The objective of this project is to document the variability of nutrients, phytoplankton, and related water quality parameters at high spatial resolution in the North Delta and Central Delta (California, USA).

idinfo.accconst: None. Please see 'Distribution Information' for details.
idinfo.useconst: hese data are marked with a Creative Common CC0 1.0 Universal License. These data are in the public domain and do not have any use constraints. Users are advised to read the dataset's metadata thoroughly to understand appropriate use and data limitations. It is requested that the suggested citation for this USGS data release be included in any publications that reference or utilize these data.
idinfo.datacred: California State Water Resources Control Board (https://www.waterboards.ca.gov/)
idinfo.native: Environment of Metadata Creation: Microsoft Windows 11 Enterprise Version 23H2 (OS Build 22631.5335). Python version 3.9.13, software libraries: pandas 0.220.

attracc.attraccr:
YSI Inc. EXO sondes were calibrated before use in the field with the KorEXO software (version 1.2.18). For calibration, the sonde sensors are submerged one at a time in standards, then the verified chemical concentration is recorded within the instrument. If the instrument measures an unknown value of the parameter, it refers to the calibration information to calculate the concentration of the chemical in the water (EXO User Manual, pg. 81).
Some software may interpret blank values as zero. Blank values should be used with caution. Values displayed in Excel may be slightly different due to default rounding in Excel. Some trailing zeroes may not be displayed if opened in Excel. It is recommended to use a text editor when viewing .txt files to avoid any rounding or formatting issues.

dataqual.logic: Redundancy is built into the system, meaning many of the parameters included in the data set are measured by 2 different instruments (e.g. EXO and Wet-Star for chlorophyll and dissolved organic matter fluorescence; EXO and Thermosalinograph for temperature and conductivity; EXO and C-Star for turbidity). Data was displayed in real-time for on the spot user verification. Topology rules were applied to points, lines, and polygons to ensure the integrity of the geospatial data.
dataqual.complete: Data set is considered complete for the information presented, as described in the abstract. Users are advised to read the rest of the metadata record carefully for additional details.

horizpa.horizpar: We conducted a visual check that the GPS data we recorded matched the boat GPS. Data was also displayed in real-time, allowing us to spot check GPS data underway. In addition, GPS location information was compared to field notes that described known locations and features. The datum used was North American Datum (NAD) 1983.

procstep.procdesc:
Surveys were conducted during daylight hours from a moving boat, the USGS research vessel (R/V) George Aiken, on May 15th through 18th, July 17th through 20th, and October 23rd and 24th, 2023, following the approach described by Bergamaschi and others (2024), Richardson and others (2023b), and Stumpner and others (2020). For analysis, sample water was continuously pumped onto the boat using two SHURflo Aqua King II pumps at a pressure of 60 psi and rate of 5 GPM (SHURflo Pentair, London, United Kingdom) while underway at speeds up to 30 miles per hour (mph) (13 meters per second [m/s]) using a pick-up tube mounted at a fixed depth of approximately 1 m below the surface. Water was routed through a 178 micron (um) in-line strainer (Cole Parmer; EW-29595-47, Vernon Hills, Illinois) to remove large debris. Sample water was split into separate flow paths as described below, and each flow path was routed through a pressure-compensated manifold that maintained system pressure at a prescribed level (~20 psi) irrespective of boat speed (Bergamaschi and others, 2024). A 2-stage debubbler was used to remove bubbles that can interfere with instrument performance. Flow rates were maintained by metering the discharge line, except for the flow path serving the open split interface, which was metered on the feed line. Flows were continuously monitored using a sight gauge (0.1-1 GPM Flow Meter LZM-15 with adjustable knob) for each flow path. A manifold delivered unfiltered water to the following flow paths: (1) A flow-through system consisting of a thermosalinograph that recorded temperature, conductance, and salinity (Sea-Bird Scientific SB45 (TSG), Bellevue, Washington); fluorometers that measured fluorescence of chlorophyll (fCHL) and fluorescence of dissolved organic matter (fDOM, Sea-Bird Scientific WETstar (WS), Philomath, Oregon); a beam transmissometer that recorded transmittance (Sea-Bird Scientific model C-Star transmissometer (CStar), Philomath, Oregon); a nitrate (NO3) analyzer (SUNA V2; Sea-Bird Scientific, Bellevue, Washington). (2) A second path provides water to a multiparameter water quality sonde (YSI EXO2) equipped with sensors to measure temperature, specific conductance (SpC), turbidity, pH, dissolved oxygen (DO), fDOM and fCHL. (3) A third path passed water to a fluorometer designed to measure different algal classes (FluoroProbe III (FP); BBE Moldaenke, Schwentinental, Germany; Eijkelkamp, 2014; Beutler and others, 2002).
In addition to water measurements, air temperature was also collected. A Campbell Scientific 108 Air Temperature sensor (Campbell Scientific, Logan, Utah) was mounted under the roof at the stern of the boat near the manifold system, 2-3 m above the surface of the water. The heat shield is not used, but it is shaded by the roof to protect from direct sunlight, and close to the cabin of the boat to protect from wind.
All instrumentation was cleaned, and calibrations were checked prior to each use following the manufacturer's recommendation. Data for most instruments were recorded at 1-second frequency on a single data logger (CR6, Campbell Scientific, Logan, Utah) together with a timestamp and boat position obtained from a high-resolution global positioning system receiver (16X-HVS, Garmin, Olathe, Kansas). The FluoroProbe logged data internally and to the host software every 1-2 seconds. All data were displayed in real time, so scientists on board could respond when they observed changes relevant to study objectives, or if issues with flow or instrument performance were noted. Data collected in situ are referred to as high resolution (HR) parameters.

Discrete water samples (DS) were collected either while underway or–in the case of more extensive sample requirements–while stopped on station. Discrete samples were collected while underway by passing water from the pickup tube through a 0.2-micron filter into a sample bottle. At designated sampling stations (approximately 10 per day) samples for determination of ammonium (NH4), nitrite (NO2), nitrate (NO3), phosphate (PO4), dissolved organic carbon (DOC), total dissolved nitrogen (TDN), and total dissolved phosphorus (TDP) concentrations were collected using this system. Additionally, unfiltered samples were collected approximately from 1 m depth using a submersible pump for analysis of chlorophyll a (Chl a), phaeophytin, particulate carbon (PC), and particulate nitrogen (PN) concentrations, and suspended sediment concentration (SSC).
Most samples were stored on wet ice in the dark during transit to the laboratory where they were stored at 2° Celsius (C). Dissolved organic carbon samples were preserved to <2 pH with high-purity sulfuric acid the day following collection and stored at 2°C. Samples for pigment analysis were filtered within 24 hours of collection and immediately frozen (-80°C). Chl a samples were filtered through a 0.7 um nominal pore size glass fiber filter (Advantec MFS, Inc, Dublin, California). Chl a associated with the larger sized cell fraction were filtered through a 5 um pore size 25 mm polycarbonate filter (General Electric Healthcare, Chicago, Illinois). Samples for determination of suspended sediment concentration (SSC), particulate carbon (PC), and particulate nitrogen (PN) were filtered through a pre-weighed 0.7 um nominal pore size combusted glass fiber filter. Frozen Chl a and SSC/PCPN filters and chilled nutrient were shipped to the Chesapeake Biological Laboratory (Solomons, Maryland).
Detailed methods documentation for analysis of discrete water quality samples can be found on the landing page (https://doi.org/10.5066/P9TW0LT5), or in metadata when sample results are downloaded from WDFN (USGS, 2025).

High-resolution (HR) SUNA NO3 data were corrected to discrete sample data. For each month, discrete NO3 concentration was subtracted from NO3 measured by the SUNA. The median difference for a given month was then subtracted from all raw SUNA NO3 values for a given month. Possible differences between SUNA and laboratory NO3 concentration data are assumed to arise from 1) actual spatial heterogeneity in environmental NO3 concentrations between water entering the flow through system and that captured in the sample bottle; 2) interferences of turbidity and dissolved organic matter using the UV absorption method for nitrate; or 3) errors in sample handling and laboratory analysis. For individual offset equations, please refer to the “DeltaMapping_2023_DataDictionary_CorrectionsAndOffsets.csv” file within the data dictionary (DeltaMapping_2023_DataDictionary.zip).
All data were processed using the pandas software library (0.220; McKinney, 2010) in Python (3.7.3; Van Rossum and Drake, 2009) to remove periods of compromised data (e.g. flow blockages, bubbles), to apply instrument corrections and unit conversions, and to apply a centered 20 second median filter to the time series.
Continuous DOC concentrations were developed by regressing onboard fDOM data against laboratory determined DOC concentrations measured in discrete samples (Snyder and others, 2018). These data are reported as estDOC. Please refer to the “DeltaMapping_2023_DataDictionary_CorrectionsAndOffsets.csv” file within the data dictionary (DeltaMapping_2023_DataDictionary.zip) for individual regression equations. Data exceeding 3 times the standard deviation of the residuals was judged to be an outlier and not included in the model.
Laboratory reported data for discrete samples (DS) from Water Data for the Nation (WDFN; USGS, 2025) are included for convenience in a discrete data file (DeltaMapping_2023_DS.txt). Remark codes, as they appear on WDFN, are included in the DS file (U.S. Geological Survey, 2011). For discrete nutrient sample concentrations, in addition to reporting nutrient parameters as they appear in WDFN (e.g., mg-N/L [milligrams nitrogen per liter]), we also reported those same parameters converted to uM (micromolar) units. For laboratory reported values that are reported below the method lowest detection limit (LDL), half of the LDL is reported in the associated uM column (Clarke, 1998). These uM data were used to bias correct the SUNA NO3 data (Pellerin and others, 2013). Table 1 summarizes mg/L to uM conversion factors and 1/2 the LDL values (Clarke, 1998).

Aside from evaluating the Delta as a whole, it is useful to divide the survey data into smaller sub-sets for analysis. We chose two main approaches to do this. First, we defined nine Study Regions based on hydrodynamically distinct zones identified by Jabusch and others (2016). Broadly speaking, the Delta has three tidal transition zones (north Delta, Mokelumne system, south Delta), two strongly tidally forced tidal lagoons (western and central Delta), two dead-end channel systems (CSC [Cache Slough Complex], and Suisun Marsh, an area we define as a complex of tidal and managed wetlands near Ryer Island), and the brackish part of the estuary that connects the Delta to San Francisco Bay (Suisun Bay). The nine regions defined in this study are (1) north Delta tidal transition zone, (2) Mokelumne tidal transition zone, (3) south Delta tidal transition zone, (4) central Delta tidal transition zone, (5) San Joaquin tidal transition zone, (6) western Delta tidal transition zone, (7) Cache Slough Complex, (8) Suisun Bay, and (9) Suisun Marsh channel system. A ‘Transition Zone’ is where transport transitions from being mostly unidirectional, like in rivers, to mostly tidal. These are similar to regions proposed by Jabusch and others (2016) to evaluate data collection efforts and nutrient status and trends analyses in the Delta. Their regions were based on Operational Landscape Units (OLUs) originally developed to represent restoration opportunity areas based on an understanding of ecological function, physical drivers, existing constraints, and elevation gradients, while taking into account hydrologic features, watershed boundaries, and DSM2 (Delta Simulation Model II) modeling requirements (Grenier and Grossinger, 2016). Second, we associated data with the geographic name of each water feature (e.g., Sacramento River, Georgiana Slough, Old River) based on widely recognized naming conventions that were cross referenced with the USGS Geographic Names Information System (GNIS) database (USGS, 2022).

enttyp.enttypd: Definitions of column headers, abbreviations, units of measure and other values included in the Delta Mapping 2023 data release
High resolution (HR), in situ, unfiltered, in vivo fluorescence (IVF), water chemistry data from the Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta Study Area collected in May 2023
High resolution, in situ, unfiltered, in vivo fluorescence (IVF), water chemistry data from the Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta Study Area collected in July 2023
High resolution, in situ, unfiltered, in vivo fluorescence (IVF), water chemistry data from the Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta Study Area collected in October 2023

overview.eaover: The data dictionary is contained in DeltaMapping_2023_DataDictionary.zip. The contained .csv files list and describe the data file field/column labels in tables. Methods are described in this metadata file, as well as DeltaMapping_2023_Methods.docx. The data release consists of .txt data files. Descriptions for each document and file are listed within this section.
overview.eadetcit: O'Donnell, K., Uebner, M.Q., Brinkman, J, Richardson, E.T., Soto Perez, J., Sturgeon, C.L., Maestas, A., Ayers, A., Nakatsuka, K., Bouma Gregson, K., Kraus, T.E.C., and Bergamaschi, B., 2025, Assessing spatial variability of nutrients, phytoplankton, and related water quality constituents in the California Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta at the landscape scale: 2023 High-resolution mapping surveys: U.S. Geological Survey data release, https://doi.org/10.5066/P9TW0LT5.

cntaddr.address: Denver Federal Center, Building 810, Mail Stop 302

distinfo.distliab: Unless otherwise stated, all data, metadata and related materials are considered to satisfy the quality standards relative to the purpose for which the data were collected. Although these data and associated metadata have been reviewed for accuracy and completeness and approved for release by the U.S. Geological Survey (USGS), no warranty expressed or implied is made regarding the display or utility of the data on any other system or for general or scientific purposes, nor shall the act of distribution constitute any such warranty. Any use of trade, product, or firm names is for descriptive purposes only and does not imply endorsement by the U.S. Government.

metainfo.metstdn: FGDC Content Standard for Digital Geospatial Metadata
